title: everyone JUST got HACKED...
summary: Wes Roth discusses recent AI security breaches and vulnerabilities including Apple's macOS exploited via Anthropic's Mythos model, Google's findings on AI-leveraged vulnerability exploitation, and Microsoft's multi-agent AI system outperforming competitors on cybersecurity benchmarks.
writeup: The video covers two concurrent AI-assisted cybersecurity incidents: Kalifi.io researchers using Claude (via Anthropic's Project Glasswing program) to discover a kernel-level local privilege escalation chain on macOS 12.6.4.1 on Apple M5 hardware with memory integrity enforcement enabled, breaking in five days what took Apple and Google engineers half a decade to build; and Google Threat Intelligence Group's May 11 disclosure of the first confirmed case of attackers deploying an AI-generated zero-day exploit in the wild against a popular open-source web administration tool. The through-line is acceleration: AI is compressing the time to find vulnerabilities in hardened systems, and the fingerprint left by the LLM in the attacker's script reveals both the capability and a forensic tell.
key_points: Kalifi.io researchers, operating under Anthropic's Project Glasswing access program, used Claude alongside two human researchers to break macOS 12.6.4.1 memory integrity enforcement on Apple M5 hardware in five days, escalating from an unprivileged local user account to root administrator privilege, a protection that Michael Zalewski (former Google security researcher) described as the culmination of half a decade of unprecedented engineering effort.

Google Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG) confirmed on May 11 the first case of attackers using an LLM to build and deploy a working zero-day exploit in the wild, specifically a Python-based attack script that bypassed two-factor authentication in a popular open-source web administration tool.

The LLM origin of the attacker's script was identified forensically: the attack code included a CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) severity rating, a detail no human attacker would include in their own malware but that the model hallucinated because its training data on known exploits associates exploit descriptions with CVSS scores.

The attacker group used agentic tools including OpenClaw and OneClaw alongside intentionally vulnerable controlled environments to refine and increase the reliability of AI-generated payloads before deployment, according to Google's disclosure.

Google's disclosure of breadcrumb details (Python code, two-factor authentication, web administration tool) creates a secondary risk: the hints may allow other threat actors to identify the specific open-source tool and reverse-engineer the exploit against unpatched installations.

Dario Amodei is cited as placing the major wave of AI-assisted exploitation 6 to 12 months out from the time of recording, with the current incidents representing early-stage activity rather than the peak.
